title: Coordination Requires a Common Cause in Quantum Theory
authors: Daniel Centeno, Antoine Coquet, Maria Ciudad Alañón, Lucas Tendick, Marc-Olivier Renou, Elie Wolfe
abstract: We propose a novel causal principle that is a genuinely multipartite extension of Reichenbach's common cause principle, namely, the coordination principle: parties in a network can achieve perfect randomized coordination--in particular, agree on a uniformly random output--only if they all share a common cause. We show that this principle does not follow from the standard no-signaling and independence principles by providing an explicit theory satisfying all these principles while violating the coordination principle. Strikingly, we prove that the coordination principle holds, however, in quantum theory for four parties, and derive noise-tolerant Bell-like inequalities that certify a common cause. We then extend these results to a genuinely quantum coordination task, showing that the four-partite GHZ state requires a quantum common cause which can also be certified by experimentally accessible Bell-like inequalities. A companion paper generalizes these results for N parties, proving that the coordination principle is satisfied in general for quantum theory.
summary: The paper demonstrates that in quantum theory, achieving perfect coordination among multiple parties is only possible if they share a common cause. It distinguishes quantum theory from other possible theories that satisfy standard causality principles but allow coordination without a common cause. Finally, it provides Bell-like inequalities to certify these common causes in noisy experimental settings.
main_problem: Investigating whether the 'Coordination Principle'—the idea that perfect randomized coordination among multiple parties requires a common cause—is a fundamental property of quantum theory or merely a consequence of No-Signaling and Independence principles.
main_result: The authors prove that quantum theory satisfies the Coordination Principle, whereas other operational probabilistic theories satisfying No-Signaling and Independence do not. They also derive noise-tolerant Bell-like inequalities to experimentally certify the presence of a common cause.
method: The authors use an Operational Probabilistic Theory (OPT) framework, employing a 'quantum inflation technique' and Sum of Squares (SOS) arguments to derive contradictions in circuits lacking a common cause.
model_or_system: A network of N parties (specifically analyzed for N=4) connected via a causal circuit involving sources, intermediate channels, and measurement nodes, involving both classical bit coordination and quantum GHZ state generation.
key_observables: Correlators for bit agreement (e.g., p(a=b)) and fidelity of the 4-partite GHZ state.
important_parameters: Fidelity threshold (v > 94.17%) for certifying a quantum common cause in a noisy GHZ state.
main_assumptions: The proof assumes that correlations in nature obey the mathematical structure of quantum theory and the Independence principle.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a causal circuit for a 4-party coordination task; Figure 2 illustrates the graphical representation of the quantum inflation process.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Coordination Principle, constructs an OPT that violates it to show NSI is insufficient, proves the principle holds for quantum theory, extends it to quantum coordination tasks (GHZ states), and provides experimental certification tools via inequalities.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for quantum information and quantum optics as it establishes a new fundamental causal principle for quantum theory and provides practical, noise-robust tools for certifying multipartite entanglement and causal structures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Coordination task between A, B, C and D with- out a common cause. Parties A, B, C and D each output a bit a, b, c and d, respectively. Their task is to attempt to coordinate their outcome, i.e., obtain a uniformly random bit a=b=c=d. The parties are assisted by shared information re- ceived according to the above circuit. Note that the circuit we describe is to be understood through a causal lens, i.e., any wire is represented by an arrow to indicate a causal influence. Addi- tionally, we consider the systems carrying information in the wires to be of arbitrary dimension. The leftmost nodes (ABC, ABD, ACD, BCD) are sources of information, the intermedi- ate nodes (AB,AC,...,CD) are...

FIG. 2. Graphical representation of the quantum infla- tion used to demonstrate that Quantum theory satisfies the Coordination Principle. The superindex in parenthesis indicate the copy index whenever there is more than one copy. The black dots highlight whenever the same Hilbert space of a given source state is pertinent in defining more than one trans- formation. Accordingly, pairs of transformations related by a black dot (AC(1) and AC(2) or BD(1) and BD(2)) cannot be physically implemented simultaneously.
